description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Clickjacking in Nearby Devices Dashboard

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application embeds an iframe from a subdomain that lacks clickjacking protection (e.g., X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors), and the embedded application uses session cookies.
prerequisites: Target application uses session cookies for authentication.
Target application is embeddable in an iframe without restrictive X-Frame-Options or CSP headers.
applicability_signals: The target URL can be loaded within an iframe.
The target application's login state persists across different subdomains or embeds.
skip_if: Target page explicitly sets 'X-Frame-Options: DENY' or 'X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN'.
CSP 'frame-ancestors' directive explicitly denies framing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Attempt to load the target URL in an iframe in a controlled HTML page to check for framing restrictions.
Step 2: Inspect the response headers (X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy) of the target URL and any embedded iframes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a sensitive action within the target application (e.g., device deletion).
Step 2: Craft an HTML page that embeds the vulnerable iframe with the sensitive action's URL.
Step 3: Overlay a deceptive UI element (e.g., a button) on top of the iframe's interactive elements using CSS positioning and z-index.
Step 4: Provide a malicious link containing the crafted HTML to a logged-in victim.
Step 5: Instruct the victim to click the deceptive UI element.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To inspect response headers and test iframe embeddability.
Self-hosted web server: To host the crafted HTML payload.
success_indicators: The sensitive action (e.g., device deletion) is performed on the victim's account without their explicit intent.
The embedded iframe successfully loads the target page with the victim's active session cookies.
failure_indicators: The iframe fails to load (e.g., due to X-Frame-Options or CSP blocking).
The embedded page prompts the victim to log in again, indicating session cookies are not being passed correctly.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trick a logged-in victim into performing sensitive actions on their behalf without their knowledge, such as deleting devices in the Google Nearby Devices Dashboard.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always apply 'X-Frame-Options: DENY' or a restrictive 'Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors' header to all sensitive application pages to prevent clickjacking attacks.

meta.title: Auth Bypass in https://nearbydevices-pa.googleapis.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access Google Cloud resources, and there is a need to perform actions on devices associated with another user's project.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Google account.
Victim must have created a GCP project and registered a Google Fast Pair device under that project.
applicability_signals: Existence of the nearbydevices-pa.googleapis.com API.
API endpoints that accept project identifiers or device identifiers as parameters.
API responses that contain sensitive information like Anti-Spoofing Private Keys.
skip_if: API responses consistently return authorization errors (e.g., 403 Forbidden) even with valid session tokens.
No identifiable project or device IDs can be discovered or manipulated.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to Google with attacker's account and navigate to the Google Fast Pair device management console (developers.google.com/nearby/devices/). Note the attacker's GCP project number.

Step 2: Authenticate to Google with a victim's account (or a second attacker-controlled account) and navigate to the Google Fast Pair device management console. Note the victim's GCP project number and register a new device (e.g., Fast Pair -> Headphones).
Step 3: Proxy traffic using Burp Suite while interacting with the victim's registered device in the console.

Step 4: Identify the gRPC request made to nearbydevices-pa.googleapis.com/location.nearby.v1.NearbyDevicesService/GetObservedDevice. Extract the Victim's Project Number and the device's Model ID (if available in the request/response).
Step 5: Alternatively, identify a REST endpoint (e.g., /v1/projects/[victim_project_number]/devices/[device_id]) that might reveal the project number associated with a device.
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure Burp Suite's Match and Replace rule to substitute the attacker's Project Number with the Victim's Project Number in the request body for requests targeting nearbydevices-pa.googleapis.com.
Step 2: Access the list of devices associated with the Victim's Project Number through the Fast Pair console or by directly querying the API.

Step 3: If the victim's device is listed, attempt to perform actions such as viewing device details, accessing the Anti-Spoofing Private Key, adding a new firmware revision, editing device information, viewing analytics, or deleting the device.

Step 4: For a real-world MITM scenario, intercept the initial pairing traffic of a target device (e.g., Pixel Buds) and extract its Model ID and Project Number from the GetObservedDevice gRPC response.

Step 5: Construct a GET request to the /v1/projects/[pixel's_project_number]/devices/[pixel's_model_id_in_decimal]?key=AIzaSyBbXGPRxbh6-U8pujg5-lABDGuThIjtn38 endpoint with the extracted identifiers and an attacker's access token to retrieve the Anti-Spoofing Private Key.
payloads_or_patterns: Burp Match and Replace rule: Type=Request body, Match=<Attacker's_Project_Number>, Replace=<Victim's_Project_Number>

GET /v1/projects/<VICTIM_PROJECT_NUMBER>/devices/<DEVICE_MODEL_ID_DECIMAL>?key=<ATTACKER_API_KEY> HTTP/1.1
Host: nearbydevices-pa.googleapis.com
Authorization: Bearer <ATTACKER_ACCESS_TOKEN>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Pro: Proxying traffic, Match and Replace functionality, Repeater for crafting requests.
protobuf decoding tools (e.g., protoc, blackboxprotobuf) - note: writeup indicates these may fail.
gRPC-aware tools for inspecting protobuf messages.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of another user's device details.
Successful retrieval of the Anti-Spoofing Private Key for a targeted device.
Ability to modify, delete, or add firmware to another user's registered device.
Successful MITM attack against a Fast Pair Bluetooth connection using the stolen Anti-Spoofing Private Key.
failure_indicators: API requests consistently return 403 Forbidden errors, indicating access control is properly enforced.
The Match and Replace rule does not affect the requests, or the target Project Number is not found in the request body.
The specified device ID or Project Number does not exist or cannot be resolved by the API.

playbooks.impact: Complete CRUD access to any Google Fast Pair device, including the ability to steal the Anti-Spoofing Private Key. This allows for device spoofing, MITM attacks on Bluetooth connections, denial-of-service by deleting devices, and defacement by changing device names/images.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that authenticated API requests are restricted to resources owned by the authenticated user, especially when dealing with sensitive identifiers like project numbers or device IDs.

meta.title: MSSQL Injection In JSON Request

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application accepts JSON payloads for sensitive operations like OTP validation and the `DeviceId` parameter is susceptible to SQL injection.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to intercept mobile application traffic (e.g., by bypassing SSL pinning).
applicability_signals: OTP validation endpoint exists and accepts JSON data.
A parameter within the JSON payload (e.g., `DeviceId`) appears to be directly reflected in database queries.
skip_if: WAF blocks requests with single quotes.
The `DeviceId` parameter is validated and sanitized server-side before being used in SQL queries.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept mobile application traffic using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) after bypassing SSL pinning.
Step 2: Identify the endpoint responsible for OTP validation and observe the structure of the JSON request.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send the intercepted OTP validation request to the Repeater tab in Burp Suite.
Step 2: Append a single quote (') to the `DeviceId` parameter's value (e.g., change `"DeviceId":"<value>"` to `"DeviceId":"<value>'"`).
Step 3: Observe the application's response for any error messages or changes in behavior that indicate SQL injection.
Step 4: If an error is observed, attempt to confirm the vulnerability using time-based SQL injection payloads for MSSQL (e.g., `WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:10'`).
Step 5: If time-based injection is successful, craft a payload to create a table (e.g., `CREATE TABLE kailash (line varchar(8000));--`).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater)
frida (for SSL pinning bypass)
success_indicators: Error messages in the response when a single quote is appended.
A noticeable delay (e.g., 10 seconds) in the response time when using time-based injection payloads.
Confirmation from the development team that a created table (e.g., `kailash`) exists.
failure_indicators: No error or change in response after appending a single quote → Parameter is likely sanitized or not directly used in SQL.
No time delay observed with `WAITFOR DELAY` → Target database might not be MSSQL, or WAF is blocking the payload.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary SQL queries on the MSSQL database, potentially leading to data exfiltration, data modification, or even remote code execution depending on database privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test JSON parameters in API requests for SQL injection vulnerabilities, especially for sensitive operations like authentication or validation, and use time-based payloads to confirm blind SQL injection.

meta.title: Edmodo Bug Bounty Writeup - XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an input field on snapshot.edmodo.com accepts user-provided data that is reflected in the page and basic XSS payloads are filtered or stripped.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on Edmodo.
applicability_signals: Presence of input fields on snapshot.edmodo.com that appear to be for form creation.
Basic XSS payloads (e.g., <script>alert(1)</script>) are blocked or sanitized by the server.
skip_if: No input fields are present on snapshot.edmodo.com.
Basic XSS payloads execute without needing evasion techniques.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to https://snapshot.edmodo.com and identify input fields where user-generated content can be submitted.
Step 2: Submit a basic XSS payload (e.g., <script>alert(1)</script>) to check if it is filtered or sanitized.
attack_steps: Step 1: If basic payloads are blocked, consult the OWASP XSS Filter Evasion Cheatsheet for advanced encoding techniques.
Step 2: Construct a Base64 encoded XSS payload within an <embed> tag.
Step 3: Submit the crafted payload to the identified input field on https://snapshot.edmodo.com.
Step 4: Observe the response for signs of script execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to analyze responses.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect DOM and identify where input is reflected.
OWASP XSS Filter Evasion Cheatsheet: For payload construction.
success_indicators: An alert box containing 'XSS' or similar text pops up in the browser.
The embedded content executes JavaScript, confirming the XSS vulnerability.
failure_indicators: The payload is rendered as plain text or sanitized HTML, and no script execution occurs.
A 4xx or 5xx error is returned upon submission of the payload, indicating a severe input validation issue.
If the payload only works in Firefox and not other browsers, consider this a partial success or a browser-specific issue. If the agent does not have Firefox, it should skip this specific payload.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on snapshot.edmodo.com. This could lead to session hijacking, phishing, credential theft, or defacement of the page.
playbooks.key_lesson: When basic XSS payloads are filtered, attempt to use encoded payloads (e.g., Base64) within less common HTML tags (e.g., <embed>) as an evasion technique.

meta.title: Mass Assignment exploitation in the wild - Escalating privileges in style

tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses role-based access control for user profiles

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows authenticated users to update their profile information via API requests that accept JSON payloads and does not properly filter properties based on an allowlist.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated user account.
Target application uses a framework susceptible to mass assignment.
applicability_signals: Endpoints exist that accept PUT/POST requests with JSON bodies for user data updates.
User profile update functionality is present.
Application relies on server-side binding of request parameters to objects.
skip_if: All API requests strictly validate and filter all incoming JSON parameters against an allowlist.
The application performs robust authorization checks on all object property updates, regardless of the source.
No identifiable user update endpoints are found.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify API endpoints that handle user profile updates, especially those that accept JSON payloads. Look for POST or PUT requests to paths like /api/users/{id}, /profile, or similar.
Step 2: Intercept a legitimate request to update a user profile. Examine the JSON payload to understand the expected structure and parameters.
Step 3: Analyze the application's behavior when different parameters are sent in the JSON payload. Observe if the application logs or provides error messages for unexpected parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: For an authenticated non-admin user, construct a PUT or POST request to the user profile update endpoint. Include the original user data.
Step 2: Modify the JSON payload by adding or changing sensitive parameters that are not typically exposed to regular users, such as 'role' or 'isAdmin'. For example: {"role": "admin"}.
Step 3: Send the modified request and observe the server's response.
Step 4: If the request is successful and the user's role is elevated, attempt to access administrator-specific functionalities.
Step 5: If direct parameter guessing fails, use tools like Burp Suite's Param Miner with the 'Guess JSON parameters' option to bruteforce common sensitive JSON parameters.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and for using extensions like Param Miner.
Postman or curl: For crafting and sending custom API requests.
success_indicators: The user's role is changed to administrator after sending the crafted request.
The response indicates a successful update, and subsequent requests to admin-only areas are now permitted.
The user gains access to administrative panels or functionalities.
failure_indicators: The server returns an error indicating an invalid parameter or an authorization failure (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 403 Forbidden). Suggestion: Try different parameter names or explore if there are other update endpoints.

The request appears successful, but the user's role or permissions do not change. Suggestion: Verify if the parameter is actually processed or if there's another layer of authorization. Explore other sensitive parameters.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can escalate their privileges from a normal authenticated user to a full administrator, allowing them to perform any action within the application, including accessing sensitive data, modifying configurations, or deleting content.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and filter all incoming JSON parameters on the server-side to prevent mass assignment vulnerabilities that could lead to privilege escalation. Never trust client-provided data for sensitive attributes like roles or permissions.

meta.title: One-click reflected XSS in www.instagram.com due to unfiltered URI schemes leads to account takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website (like Instagram) does not properly filter URI schemes in user-supplied input, allowing for the injection of JavaScript payloads that can be executed in the context of the user's browser.
prerequisites: Target accepts user input that can be rendered or processed as a URI.
applicability_signals: The presence of user-controllable links or input fields that are expected to handle URIs.
Observing that the application does not sanitize or validate the protocol of provided URIs.
skip_if: All URI schemes are strictly validated and only whitelisted protocols (e.g., http, https) are permitted.
A robust Content Security Policy (CSP) prevents inline scripts and the execution of external scripts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse www.instagram.com and identify any features that allow users to input or generate URLs, such as profile bio, posts, or direct messages.
Step 2: Attempt to provide custom URI schemes (e.g., `javascript:alert(1)`, `vbscript:msgbox('XSS')`) in these input fields and observe the application's response and rendering behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL that leverages an unfiltered URI scheme, for example, `instagram://user?username=<TARGET_USERNAME>`. If the application redirects to a specific user's profile based on this URI, it might be vulnerable.
Step 2: If the above step indicates potential vulnerability, construct a malicious payload using the `javascript:` scheme. For example, `javascript:alert(document.domain)`.

Step 3: Craft a URL that embeds the `javascript:` payload. The exact structure will depend on the vulnerable component. A common pattern for reflected XSS is `https://www.instagram.com/<some_vulnerable_parameter>=javascript:alert(1)`.
Step 4: Send this crafted URL to a victim user. If they click on it and the JavaScript executes, XSS is confirmed.

Step 5: To achieve account takeover, craft a payload that steals session cookies or performs actions on behalf of the user, such as initiating a password reset or changing account settings, by making requests from the victim's browser.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to set up a Collaborator server for capturing exfiltrated data.
success_indicators: An alert box appearing with the domain name (`www.instagram.com`) when the crafted URL is clicked by the victim.
Receipt of the victim's session cookies at the attacker's controlled server or Collaborator.
Successful execution of unauthorized actions on the victim's account (e.g., changing email, password, posting content).
failure_indicators: The URI is sanitized or redirected to a safe URL without executing JavaScript. This indicates proper filtering.
A Content Security Policy (CSP) header blocks the execution of the injected JavaScript. The agent should then explore other XSS vectors or bypass techniques.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of Instagram users. This can lead to unauthorized access to private messages, photos, and personal information, as well as the ability to post content or impersonate the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-supplied URI schemes, ensuring only trusted protocols (like http, https) are allowed to prevent XSS vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Blind XSS on Google Internal System

affected_components: Google supplier portal input fields, tracked by GUTS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can submit data to input fields on a Google internal system's supplier portal that is tracked by the GUTS ticketing system.
prerequisites: Attacker must have authenticated access to the Google supplier portal.
applicability_signals: Presence of arbitrary input fields on the Google supplier portal.
skip_if: Input fields sanitize or reject XSS payloads.
No observable callback from XSS hunter within a reasonable timeframe.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify input fields on the Google supplier portal.
Step 2: Use an XSS payload from a service like xsshunter to test each input field.
attack_steps: Step 1: Submit a blind XSS payload to various input fields on the Google supplier portal.
Step 2: Monitor the XSS hunter dashboard for any callbacks.
Step 3: If a callback is received, attempt to extract information from the response (Referer and Cookies were available).
payloads_or_patterns: Payload from xsshunter (exact payload not specified in writeup)
tools_recommended: xsshunter
Browser for submitting requests
success_indicators: A callback received in the xsshunter dashboard.
Observable data in the callback response (e.g., Referer, Cookies).
failure_indicators: No callback received after a significant delay.
XSS hunter loading indefinitely or browser freezing due to payload execution attempt.
403 Forbidden responses or input validation errors.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of another user browsing the Google internal system, potentially leading to data exfiltration, session hijacking, or unauthorized actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing for blind XSS, always use a reliable callback service and be prepared to analyze available data like Referer and Cookies to gain context, as direct DOM inspection may be hindered.

meta.title: Counter-Strike Global Offsets: reliable remote code execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a player connects to a custom or community-hosted CS:GO server that is under attacker control.
prerequisites: Attacker can host a custom CS:GO server.
Target player connects to the attacker-controlled server.
applicability_signals: Server can send `CSVCMsg_SplitScreen` messages to the client.
The `slot` field in `CSVCMsg_SplitScreen` is used as an array index without bounds checking.
skip_if: Server cannot send `CSVCMsg_SplitScreen` messages.
The `slot` field is properly validated for array bounds before use.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze CS:GO network protocol and Protobuf message definitions. Use resources like SteamDatabase GitHub.
Step 2: Develop a CS:GO proxy to capture, decrypt, unpack, and modify network traffic between client and server.
Step 3: Identify the `CSVCMsg_SplitScreen` Protobuf message and its fields (`type`, `slot`, `player_index`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a `CSVCMsg_SplitScreen` message with a manipulated `slot` field. The value of `slot` should be chosen to cause an out-of-bounds access on the splitscreen player objects array in `engine.dll`.
Step 2: Send this crafted message from the malicious community server to the target player's game client.
Step 3: The out-of-bounds access leads to a controllable virtual function call, allowing for control over the instruction pointer and subsequent RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload: Exploit primitive to achieve control over instruction pointer via controlled VFT call.
tools_recommended: Custom CS:GO proxy (developed from reverse-engineering)
Protobuf serialization/deserialization library
Debugger and disassembler (e.g., Ghidra, IDA Pro) for deep analysis of `engine.dll`
success_indicators: Client crashes with specific access violation errors pointing to the exploited memory region.
Execution of attacker-controlled code within the CS:GO client process.
Observable changes in game behavior indicating code execution (e.g., chat messages appearing without player input, visual glitches).
failure_indicators: No crash or abnormal behavior from the client. The `slot` value might be validated by the server or client, or the memory layout might differ.
Crash occurs but not in a controllable manner (e.g., segmentation fault without a clear VFT overwrite). Suggest pivoting to other message types or finding the ASLR bypass first.

playbooks.impact: Reliable remote code execution on the player's machine when connecting to a malicious server. This can lead to full compromise of the player's system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always perform strict bounds checking on array indices derived from untrusted input, especially when dealing with complex network protocols and game clients.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exposes a file retrieval endpoint that is vulnerable to Local File Inclusion (LFI) and does not properly sanitize user-supplied file paths.
prerequisites: The target application must be using SearchBlox.
The application must have the `/searchblox/servlet/FileServlet` endpoint accessible.
applicability_signals: The presence of a URL parameter that accepts file paths or URLs.
The `/searchblox/servlet/FileServlet` endpoint is reachable.
skip_if: The `/searchblox/servlet/FileServlet` endpoint returns a 404 or other error indicating it's not present.
The application performs strict validation on the `url` parameter, preventing traversal or known sensitive file paths.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the `/searchblox/servlet/FileServlet` endpoint on the target application.
Step 2: Test the `url` parameter with common Linux file paths like `/etc/passwd` to confirm LFI vulnerability.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to `/searchblox/servlet/FileServlet?url=/etc/passwd&col=9` (or other valid `col` values) to confirm basic LFI.
Step 2: Attempt to read the SearchBlox configuration file by sending a GET request to `/searchblox/servlet/FileServlet?url=/opt/tomcat/webapps/searchblox/WEB-INF/config.xml&col=1`.
Step 3: If the `config.xml` file is readable, analyze its contents for sensitive information such as database credentials, API keys, or other configuration details.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use Repeater to send crafted GET requests and analyze responses.
curl: For scripting and automating requests.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of the `/etc/passwd` file contents.
Successful retrieval of the `/opt/tomcat/webapps/searchblox/WEB-INF/config.xml` file contents.
Extraction of sensitive information (credentials, keys) from the `config.xml` file.
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found or other error responses for `/searchblox/servlet/FileServlet`.
Generic error messages or empty responses when requesting sensitive files.
The `col` parameter is invalid or not provided, leading to no file content being returned.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary file read on the server, potentially leading to disclosure of sensitive configuration files, credentials, or other confidential data. In this specific case, reading `config.xml` could reveal application secrets.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test file inclusion parameters with known sensitive files like `/etc/passwd` and application-specific configuration files (e.g., `config.xml`) to uncover LFI vulnerabilities.

meta.title: CVE-2021-27075: Microsoft Azure Vulnerability Allows Privilege Escalation and Leak of Private Data

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an unprivileged user can interact with or influence the configuration of Azure VM extensions on a target Linux VM.
prerequisites: Target is an Azure VM running Linux.
The Azure VM Agent (WAAgent) is installed and running.
The attacker has a low-privileged user account on the target VM.
applicability_signals: The presence of the `/var/lib/waagent` directory.
The ability to make HTTP requests to `168.63.129.16` (Wire Server/Instance Metadata Service).
skip_if: WAF blocks requests to `168.63.129.16`.
VM Agent is not installed or configured incorrectly.
The attacker lacks any shell access to the VM.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target is an Azure VM by checking for the existence of the `/var/lib/waagent` directory and if non-root users have read permissions on any files within it.
Step 2: Verify network connectivity from the VM to the Azure Instance Metadata Service IP address (168.63.129.16) by attempting to fetch the GoalState endpoint.
attack_steps: Step 1: As an unprivileged user, send an HTTP GET request to `http://168.63.129.16/machine/plugins?comp=waagent&type=update_discovery&rid=<VM_RESOURCE_ID>` to fetch the GoalState.
Step 2: Parse the GoalState response to extract the URL for the `ExtensionsConfig`.
Step 3: Query the `ExtensionsConfig` URL to retrieve the configuration details of all installed VM extensions.
Step 4: Within the `ExtensionsConfig`, locate the `protectedSettings` field for any VM extension.
Step 5: If `protectedSettings` contains sensitive data (e.g., API keys, credentials), extract and exfiltrate this information.
payloads_or_patterns: Parsing the JSON response for fields like "publicSettings" and "protectedSettings" within extension configurations.
tools_recommended: curl: To make HTTP requests to `168.63.129.16` and fetch configuration files.
jq: For parsing JSON responses from the API.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of `protectedSettings` containing sensitive data such as API keys, credentials, or other secrets related to VM extensions.
The ability to exfiltrate this data to an attacker-controlled server.
failure_indicators: Receiving network errors or timeouts when trying to reach `168.63.129.16`. (Suggests network restrictions or incorrect VM configuration.)
Empty or malformed GoalState/ExtensionsConfig responses. (Suggests issues with WAAgent or Azure API.)
No sensitive information found in `protectedSettings`. (Suggests that specific extensions may not store secrets in this field or they are properly encrypted/obfuscated.)

playbooks.impact: An unprivileged user can potentially leak private data associated with any Azure VM extension, including sensitive credentials, API keys, and configuration details. This information could then be used to escalate privileges on the VM, perform lateral movement within the Azure environment, or compromise other services that rely on the leaked credentials.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the communication channels and configuration endpoints used by cloud VM agents and extensions, as they often contain sensitive secrets and can be targets for information disclosure and privilege escalation.

meta.title: 2FA Verification Bypass in Shapeshift

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user has 2FA enabled and is attempting to log in.
prerequisites: Attacker must have access to a compromised account with 2FA enabled, or the ability to initiate a login for a target account.
The target application's 2FA implementation must be vulnerable to parameter tampering.
applicability_signals: The presence of a 2FA verification step during the login process.
The ability to intercept and modify requests during the 2FA verification.
skip_if: 2FA codes are validated server-side with strong, unbypassable checks.
No discernible parameters are exposed during the 2FA verification process that can be tampered with.

recon_steps: Step 1: Register an account on shapeshift.com.
Step 2: Enable 2FA for the registered account.
Step 3: Attempt to log in with the 2FA enabled account and observe the requests and responses during the 2FA verification step.
Step 4: Identify parameters within the 2FA verification request that can be manipulated.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a login to the target account (potentially a compromised account or a target user's account if credentials are known).
Step 2: When presented with the 2FA verification prompt, intercept the subsequent request.
Step 3: Tamper with a specific parameter in the 2FA verification request (details are missing in the writeup, but likely involves changing a code, a flag, or a status).
Step 4: Submit the tampered request.
Step 5: Observe if the login is successful without providing the correct 2FA code.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) for intercepting and modifying requests.
success_indicators: Successful login to the account without providing the correct 2FA code.
The attacker gains full access to the account.
failure_indicators: Login fails with an error message indicating invalid 2FA code.
Request is rejected with a 4xx status code due to invalid parameters.
The application requires the correct 2FA code, indicating the bypass was not successful.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain unauthorized access to user accounts that have 2FA enabled without needing to provide the valid two-factor authentication code.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the integrity and origin of all parameters involved in authentication and multi-factor authentication flows, not just the primary credentials.

meta.title: Identify a Facebook user by his phone number despite privacy settings set

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user's phone number is known or can be guessed, and privacy settings might be circumvented.
prerequisites: Attacker has a way to interact with Facebook's user lookup or friend suggestion features.
applicability_signals: Observing Facebook's API or UI behavior when a phone number is provided in a search or lookup context.
skip_if: Facebook robustly validates phone number privacy settings and does not expose associated user information.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints or user interfaces where a phone number can be submitted for lookup or association.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic during operations involving phone number input to understand how Facebook processes this data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use a known or guessed phone number as input to a Facebook feature that typically associates numbers with accounts (e.g., 'find friends', account recovery).
Step 2: Observe the response from Facebook. If the phone number is linked to an account, look for any disclosure of user information, even if limited, that would not otherwise be visible.
Step 3: If direct disclosure is not observed, attempt to use the phone number in conjunction with other features (e.g., password reset, profile lookup) to infer user identity.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses related to phone number submissions.
success_indicators: Receiving any identifiable information about a Facebook user (e.g., name, profile picture, friend list) when only a phone number was provided, especially if privacy settings should prevent this.
failure_indicators: Facebook consistently returns generic responses or errors, indicating no account is found or no information is disclosed for the given phone number. Pivot: Explore other phone number related features or test with variations of phone number formats.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of a Facebook user's identity (name, profile picture, etc.) based solely on their phone number, even if privacy settings are configured to prevent such visibility. This can lead to unauthorized contact, stalking, or further social engineering attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When handling sensitive user data like phone numbers, ensure that all privacy settings are strictly enforced across all related features and APIs to prevent information leakage.

meta.title: CVE-2021-1815 – MacOS Local Privilege Escalation Via Preferences

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can interact with the cfprefsd daemon and the system uses periodic maintenance scripts configured via /etc/defaults/periodic.conf.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated access to a macOS system.
The target macOS version is vulnerable and has not been patched against CVE-2021-1815.
applicability_signals: The presence of the /etc/defaults/periodic.conf file.
The cfprefsd daemon is running with root privileges.
The cfprefsd daemon accepts XPC connections from user processes.
skip_if: The system has been patched to fix CVE-2021-1815.
Periodic maintenance scripts are disabled or not configured.
The attacker lacks authenticated access to the target macOS system.

recon_steps: Step 1: Confirm running macOS version and check for patches related to CVE-2020-9839 and CVE-2021-1815.
Step 2: Verify the existence and content of '/etc/defaults/periodic.conf'.
Step 3: Confirm that the cfprefsd daemon is running and accessible via XPC.
attack_steps: Step 1: Establish an XPC connection to the 'com.apple.cfprefsd.daemon' service.

Step 2: Instruct the cfprefsd daemon to create the directory '/usr/local/etc/periodic' (or a similar user-defined periodic script directory specified in periodic.conf) and set its ownership to the current user.
Step 3: Create a malicious script within the newly created and owned directory (e.g., '/usr/local/etc/periodic/daily/malicious_script').
Step 4: Wait for the periodic maintenance scripts to execute (daily, weekly, or monthly, depending on the script's placement).
payloads_or_patterns: XPC message to cfprefsd to create directory and set ownership: Needs to be constructed using xpc libraries, as shown in the provided C code example. The specific message details are omitted but involve parameters for directory path and ownership. Example snippet from writeup: `conn = xpc_connection_create_mach_service(serviceName, NULL, XPC_CONNECTION_MACH_SERVICE_PRIVILEGED);`

Malicious script content: e.g., `#!/bin/bash
echo 'Privilege Escalation Successful!' > /tmp/pwned.txt
exit 0`
tools_recommended: C compiler (e.g., clang) for creating XPC interaction tool.
macOS command-line tools for file manipulation and script creation.
success_indicators: The malicious script executes with root privileges (e.g., by creating a file in /root or modifying a system configuration file).
Observing the output or side effects of the malicious script.
failure_indicators: XPC connection to cfprefsd fails.
cfprefsd refuses to create the directory or set ownership.
Periodic scripts do not execute or do not execute the malicious script with root privileges.
Possible pivot: If direct directory creation and ownership change fails, investigate other ways to influence cfprefsd's file operations or other daemons handling user preferences.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve arbitrary code execution with root privileges on the target macOS system by leveraging the ability to place and execute a script that runs as part of the root-privileged periodic maintenance tasks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always re-evaluate patch effectiveness for privilege escalation vulnerabilities, especially those involving daemons that interact with the file system and custom configurations.

meta.title: How I Hacked Google App Engine: Anatomy of a Java Bytecode Exploit

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application running on Google App Engine allows for the dynamic loading or manipulation of Java classes or the use of reflection, and the sandbox's security mechanisms for these features have flaws.
prerequisites: Ability to deploy custom Java code to Google App Engine.
applicability_signals: The target application is written in Java and deployed on Google App Engine.
The application utilizes or exposes functionalities related to Java reflection or custom classloaders.
App Engine's security wrapper functions for reflection or classloaders are not sufficiently robust against all possible attack vectors.
skip_if: Google App Engine has fully patched all known vulnerabilities in its bytecode rewriting and wrapper function mechanisms for reflection and classloaders.
The application does not involve any dynamic class loading or reflection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the deployed Java application code on Google App Engine to identify any usage of Java reflection APIs (e.g., `setAccessible`, `getMethod`, `invoke`) or custom classloaders.

Step 2: Research known vulnerabilities or bypass techniques related to Java bytecode manipulation, reflection, and classloader security within sandboxed environments, specifically for Google App Engine if available.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft malicious Java bytecode that attempts to bypass App Engine's security wrappers for reflection or classloaders. This might involve finding edge cases or flaws in the wrapper logic.

Step 2: For reflection bypass, attempt to invoke forbidden methods or access restricted fields by carefully constructing method names, parameters, or field names that might be misinterpreted or mishandled by the wrappers.

Step 3: For classloader bypass, attempt to define or load malicious classes that can execute arbitrary code, potentially by exploiting flaws in how App Engine's wrappers handle the byte array input or the newly defined classes.
Step 4: Deploy the crafted malicious Java application to Google App Engine.
Step 5: Monitor the deployed application for signs of successful arbitrary code execution on the Google server.
tools_recommended: Java Development Kit (JDK) for compiling Java code.
Bytecode manipulation tools (e.g., ASM, Javassist) for crafting malicious bytecode.
Google Cloud SDK for deploying applications to App Engine.
success_indicators: Gaining arbitrary code execution on the Google App Engine server, allowing for shell access or execution of arbitrary commands.
Observable signs of a successful sandbox escape, such as unexpected network callbacks from the App Engine instance or file system access where it should be restricted.
failure_indicators: The application fails to deploy, or deploys but crashes immediately with security exceptions, indicating App Engine's static analysis or wrappers have successfully blocked the malicious bytecode.
The application runs but exhibits no signs of code execution beyond its intended functionality, suggesting the exploit attempt was unsuccessful.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution on Google's servers, allowing an attacker to potentially compromise other applications, access sensitive data, or gain further access to Google's infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly analyze and test the security wrappers and bytecode rewriting mechanisms implemented by sandboxing environments, as flaws in these intermediate layers can lead to full system compromise.

meta.title: Account takeover of Instagram accounts due to unrestricted permissions of third-party application’s generated tokens

affected_components: Third-party application token generation and permission handling

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a third-party application is integrated with Facebook/Instagram using OAuth and generates tokens with potentially excessive permissions.
prerequisites: Attacker has a Facebook/Instagram account.
A third-party application uses Facebook/Instagram OAuth for authentication and authorization.
applicability_signals: Third-party applications requesting broad permissions (e.g., 'manage_pages', 'read_insights', full profile access) during OAuth authorization.
Tokens generated by third-party apps allow access to sensitive user data or actions on behalf of the user without explicit user consent for each action.
skip_if: OAuth flow enforces granular, user-specific consent for each permission.
Third-party application tokens have very limited scope and are restricted to specific, non-sensitive actions.

recon_steps: Identify third-party applications integrated with the attacker's Facebook/Instagram account.
Review the permissions granted to these third-party applications through account settings.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the OAuth flow for a target third-party application that requests broad permissions.
Step 2: Observe the generated access token and its associated permissions.

Step 3: Attempt to use the generated token to access sensitive data or perform unauthorized actions on Instagram/Facebook, which may include accessing profile information, posts, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
payloads_or_patterns: Use the generated <SESSION_TOKEN> (OAuth access token) in API requests to Facebook/Instagram endpoints that should be restricted.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and analyze OAuth token requests and responses.
Facebook Graph API Explorer: Test API calls with various tokens and permissions.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of sensitive user data (e.g., private profile details, friends list, private messages) that the user did not explicitly authorize the third-party app to access.
Unauthorized actions performed on the user's behalf (e.g., posting content, changing settings) via the third-party application's token.
failure_indicators: API requests return 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden, indicating insufficient permissions for the requested action.
The generated token only allows access to a very limited set of non-sensitive data.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized access to an Instagram user's account by exploiting the broad permissions granted to a compromised or malicious third-party application. This can lead to data theft, impersonation, and further malicious activities within the compromised account.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing OAuth integrations, scrutinize the scope of permissions requested by third-party applications and verify that access tokens generated by these applications are strictly limited to the explicitly authorized actions.

meta.title: ExifTool CVE-2021-22204 - Arbitrary Code Execution

tech_stack_fingerprints: ExifTool library used for metadata stripping

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses ExifTool to process uploaded image files and allows for custom metadata manipulation or parsing.
prerequisites: The target application must be using a vulnerable version of ExifTool (prior to 12.24).
The application must allow users to upload image files.
The application must use ExifTool to process these uploaded files.
applicability_signals: Application uses ExifTool for image processing.
ExifTool version in use is older than 12.24.
skip_if: The application explicitly sanitizes or disallows DjVu file uploads.
The application uses a patched version of ExifTool.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application processes uploaded images using ExifTool by examining HTTP requests/responses during file uploads and looking for ExifTool-related headers or content.
Step 2: Attempt to determine the version of ExifTool in use. If not directly available, check for known CVEs related to older versions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious DjVu file that exploits the CVE-2021-22204 vulnerability. This involves embedding a Perl `eval` payload within the DjVu's annotation section (ANTa chunk).
Step 2: Upload the crafted malicious DjVu file to the target application.
Step 3: The vulnerable ExifTool instance will parse the malicious DjVu, leading to the execution of the embedded Perl code, resulting in Arbitrary Code Execution on the server.
payloads_or_patterns: A specially crafted DjVu file with a malicious ANTa chunk containing `eval($ENV{IFS})` or other command execution payloads. Example structure might involve embedding a Perl `eval` statement within a quoted string in the DjVu's annotation syntax that gets processed by the `ParseAnt` method.
tools_recommended: Python script to craft malicious DjVu files.
Hex editor to inspect and modify DjVu file structure.
Burp Suite to intercept and analyze file upload requests.
success_indicators: Remote command execution confirmed (e.g., receiving a shell, executing a command like `id` and seeing the server's user ID, or exfiltrating data via a reverse shell).
Application crashes or shows unexpected behavior directly attributable to the crafted file.
failure_indicators: File upload rejected with an error indicating unsupported file type.
ExifTool processes the file without executing the payload (e.g., no RCE indicators observed). Suggestion: Verify ExifTool version and DjVu file structure, or try alternative RCE vectors if available.
Application returns a generic error for invalid file format without specific DjVu rejection.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary Code Execution on the server hosting the GitLab instance, allowing an attacker to take full control of the server, access sensitive data, and potentially pivot to other systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that server-side applications utilizing libraries for file parsing (like ExifTool) are updated to the latest stable versions to patch known vulnerabilities, especially those leading to code execution.

meta.title: HackerOne report

tech_stack_fingerprints: JavaScript enabled check on HackerOne website

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when the target's web application requires JavaScript to be enabled for full functionality, and the specific vulnerability being targeted is RCE within the GitLab platform.
prerequisites: JavaScript must be enabled in the user's browser.
applicability_signals: The website displays a message indicating JavaScript is disabled and instructs the user to enable it.
